lineitem.notes: Largest table in TPC-H, primary fact table. All queries touch this table. Date range filters are highly selective (typically 1-5% of data). Columns l_extendedprice and l_discount are always accessed together for revenue calculations.

orders.notes: Secondary fact table. Accessed only in Q3. Date filter on o_orderdate is selective (~10-20%). Join key o_orderkey connects to lineitem.

customer.notes: Dimension table. Accessed only in Q3. Filter on c_mktsegment is moderately selective (1/5 = 20% selectivity). Small enough to fit entirely in cache.

nation.notes: Tiny dimension table. Not accessed in current query set but available for foreign key joins.

region.notes: Tiny dimension table. Not accessed in current query set.

supplier.notes: Small dimension table. Not accessed in current query set but part of schema.

part.notes: Medium dimension table. Not accessed in current query set.

partsupp.notes: Bridge table connecting part and supplier. Not accessed in current query set.

joins.notes: Foreign key join in Q3. Build on filtered customer (c_mktsegment filter reduces to ~30K rows), probe with orders. Expected 1:10 fanout (each customer has ~10 orders).
Foreign key join in Q3. This is the critical join - lineitem is very large. Orders filtered by date (~150K qualifying orders), each order has ~4 lineitems. Total intermediate size ~600K rows.

filters.notes: Date range filter. Selects ~98% of data (all dates up to Sept 1998). Low selectivity but enables partition pruning if data is sorted by date.
Start of date range for year 1994. Highly selective (~1.4% of data for 1 year out of 7 years TPC-H).
End of date range for year 1994. Combined with >= creates 1-year window.
Range filter on discount (0.05 to 0.07). Assuming uniform distribution, selects ~2/11 = 18% of data. Can benefit from zone maps.
Filter for quantity < 24. Moderate selectivity (~48% assuming uniform 1-50 distribution).
Filter for shipdate after March 15, 1995. Highly selective, combined with orders.o_orderdate creates correlation opportunity.
Equality filter on market segment 'BUILDING'. Selectivity 1/5 = 20% (5 standard market segments in TPC-H).
Date filter for orders before March 15, 1995. Highly selective. Creates correlated filter with l_shipdate (order must be before, shipment after).

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality GROUP BY (l_returnflag has 3 values: A, N, R; l_linestatus has 2: O, F). Trivial hash aggregation, hash table fits in L1 cache. Perfect candidate for thread-local aggregation with merge.
Medium-high cardinality GROUP BY (one group per qualifying order). Hash table needs ~10MB for 150K groups. Pre-sizing critical to avoid rehashing. TOP-K optimization possible due to LIMIT 10.

ordering.notes: Trivial sort - only 4 groups. Can use insertion sort or even manual ordering since result is tiny.
Sort on computed column (revenue) DESC, then o_orderdate. LIMIT 10 enables top-K heap optimization - no need to fully sort 150K groups, just maintain heap of size 10.

optimization_opportunities: CRITICAL: Parallel scan of lineitem table - all 3 queries scan 6M rows, ideal for morsel-driven parallelism with 10K row chunks. Expected 7-8x speedup on 8 cores.
Columnar storage layout for lineitem - queries access only 5-8 columns out of 16 total. Columnar layout reduces memory bandwidth by 50-70% and enables SIMD vectorization.
Filter pushdown in Q6 - combine all 4 filters (shipdate range, discount range, quantity) into single vectorized scan loop before materializing any intermediate results.
Operator fusion in Q1 - fuse scan + filter + aggregation into single tight loop. Avoids materializing filtered rows, keeps working set in L2/L3 cache.
Zone maps on l_shipdate - all queries filter by date. Store min/max shipdate per chunk (e.g., per 100K rows) to skip entire chunks without scanning.

Hash join optimization for Q3 - build hash table on filtered customer (only 30K rows after c_mktsegment filter), then probe with orders. Second join builds on customer-orders result, probes with lineitem.
Partition-based parallel join in Q3 - hash-partition customer and orders by c_custkey, join partitions independently. Then partition result and lineitem by l_orderkey for second join.
Top-K heap for Q3 - instead of sorting all ~150K groups, maintain min-heap of size 10 during aggregation. Reduces sort complexity from O(n log n) to O(n log 10).
Thread-local aggregation for Q1 and Q3 - each thread maintains local hash table, merge at end. Eliminates lock contention on shared hash table.
SIMD vectorization for revenue calculation - expression l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) appears in all 3 queries. Process 4-8 values at once with AVX2.
Late materialization in Q3 - filter lineitem on l_shipdate first to get qualifying row IDs, then fetch l_extendedprice and l_discount only for matching rows.
Bloom filter for Q3 joins - build bloom filter on customer.c_custkey after market segment filter. Use to skip 80% of non-matching orders before hash probe.
Correlated filter exploitation in Q3 - orders with o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' and lineitem with l_shipdate > '1995-03-15' creates constraint. Can pre-filter orders to avoid join work.
Dictionary encoding for l_returnflag and l_linestatus - only 3 and 2 distinct values respectively. Replace CHAR(1) with uint8 codes, reduces memory by 50% and enables faster comparisons.
Pre-sizing hash tables - Q3 aggregation needs ~150K groups. Pre-allocate hash table with size = 150000 / 0.75 = 200K slots to avoid expensive rehashing mid-query.

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: All 3 queries scan lineitem. With morsel size of 10K rows and 8 threads, expect 7.5-8x speedup. Memory bandwidth is unlikely bottleneck since queries access only 30-50% of columns. I/O throughput: ~600MB/s single-threaded becomes ~4.5GB/s parallel.

Q1 aggregates 6M rows into 4 groups. Thread-local hash tables (4 groups * 8 threads = 32 total groups) with final merge step. Aggregation is CPU-bound, expect near-linear speedup. Merge phase is trivial with only 32 groups total.

Q3 three-way join. Partition-based parallel join: (1) hash-partition customer and orders by c_custkey into 64 partitions, join independently; (2) partition intermediate result and lineitem by l_orderkey into 64 partitions. Each partition is ~50K rows, fits in L3 cache (8MB). Expected 6-7x speedup with 8 threads (some overhead from partitioning and data redistribution).

Q3 aggregates ~600K rows (customer-orders-lineitem join result) into ~150K groups. Parallel hash aggregation with thread-local tables. Each thread processes ~75K rows, builds ~19K groups locally. Final merge combines 8 * 19K = 152K groups. Expected 6-7x speedup.

column_co_access_patterns.notes: These two columns are ALWAYS accessed together for revenue computation: l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount). Strong candidate for column group storage or pre-computed materialized view.
Q1 computes l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) * (1 + l_tax). These 3 columns form tight computation cluster.
Q1 groups by both. Low cardinality makes them ideal for dictionary encoding and contiguous storage.
Q1 aggregates all three (SUM, AVG). Co-locating these enables vectorized accumulation.
Q3 outputs both alongside aggregated revenue. Should be co-located since they're fetched together.

critical_path_analysis.reasoning: Q3 is the most complex query with highest execution cost. It performs: (1) Three-way join across customer (150K), orders (1.5M), and lineitem (6M rows); (2) Multiple selective filters creating intermediate results; (3) Hash aggregation into 150K groups; (4) Sort on computed column with LIMIT. Estimated cost breakdown: lineitem scan (60%), joins (25%), aggregation (10%), sort (5%).
optimization_priority: 1. Parallel scan of lineitem with date filter (l_shipdate > '1995-03-15') - reduces input from 6M to ~3M rows (50% reduction)
2. Build hash table on filtered customer (30K rows after c_mktsegment filter) - small build side is critical for performance
3. Apply bloom filter from customer keys to orders before join - skips ~80% of non-matching orders
4. Partition-based parallel join for customer-orders and result-lineitem - enables 8-way parallelism on largest join
5. Top-K heap optimization for final sort - maintain heap of 10 elements instead of sorting 150K groups
critical_path_analysis.expected_improvement: Combined optimizations should achieve 15-20x speedup: 8x from parallelism, 2x from join ordering and bloom filters, 1.3x from top-K heap.

summary: TPC-H workload is dominated by analytical scans of the large lineitem fact table (6M rows). All 3 queries are read-only with high parallelism potential. Q1 is a simple scan-filter-aggregate (trivial aggregation into 4 groups), Q6 is a selective scan with 4 range filters, and Q3 is a complex 3-way join with medium-cardinality aggregation and top-K sorting. Primary optimization opportunities: (1) Morsel-driven parallel scans for 7-8x speedup; (2) Columnar storage to reduce memory bandwidth by 60%; (3) Partition-based parallel joins for Q3; (4) Zone maps on l_shipdate for fast chunk pruning; (5) Thread-local aggregation to avoid lock contention. The workload strongly favors columnar storage, vectorized execution, and multi-core parallelism. Estimated total speedup from full optimization: 20-30x over naive single-threaded row-oriented implementation.
